subject: ap-chemistry
title: Na₂CO₃ Analysis: Precipitation, Conductivity & Buffer Chemistry
stimulus:
A student is given 50.0 mL of a solution of Na
CO
of unknown concentration. To determine the concentration
of the solution, the student mixes the solution with excess 1.0 M $Ca(NO_3)_2$(aq), causing a precipitate to form.
The balanced equation for the reaction is shown below.
Na CO (aq) + $Ca(NO_3)_2$(aq) → 2 NaNO (aq) + CaCO (s)
2 3 3 2 3 3

parts.prompt:
Write the net ionic equation for the reaction that occurs when the solutions of Na
CO
and $Ca(NO_3)_2$are
mixed.
parts.scoring_notes: All soluble ionic compounds dissociate. Spectator ions are Na$^+$ and NO$_3^-$. CaCO$_3$ is insoluble (precipitate). Net ionic equation: Ca$^{2+}$(aq) + CO$_3^{2-}$(aq) → CaCO$_3$(s).

scoring_points.description: For the correct net ionic equation: Ca$^{2+}$ + CO$_3^{2-}$ → CaCO$_3$(s): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$\text{Ca}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + \text{CO}_3^{2-}(\text{aq}) \rightarrow \text{CaCO}_3(\text{s})$
Na$^+$ and NO$_3^-$ are spectator ions (remain in solution, not in the precipitate).

wrong_examples: Including Na$^+$ or NO$_3^-$ in the equation — spectators must be cancelled.
Writing CaCO$_3$ as aqueous — CaCO$_3$ is insoluble and precipitates (solid state symbol required).
common_traps: Net ionic equation: cancel spectator ions (those that appear the same on both sides). Na$^+$ and NO$_3^-$ are spectators. Only Ca$^{2+}$ and CO$_3^{2-}$ are involved in the reaction.

parts.prompt:
The diagram below is incomplete. Draw in the species needed to accurately represent the major ionic species
remaining in the solution after the reaction has been completed.
The student filters and dries the precipitate of CaCO
(molar mass 100.1 g/mol) and records the data in the table
below.
Volume of Na CO solution 50.0 mL
Volume of 1.0 M $Ca(NO_3)_2$added 100.0 mL
Mass of CaCO precipitate collected 0.93 g
parts.scoring_notes: After the reaction, all CO$_3^{2-}$ has precipitated as CaCO$_3$ (Ca$^{2+}$ is in excess). The solution contains: Na$^+$ (from Na$_2$CO$_3$), excess Ca$^{2+}$ (from excess Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$), and NO$_3^-$ (from Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$). The particulate diagram should show Na$^+$, Ca$^{2+}$, and NO$_3^-$ ions in solution (no CO$_3^{2-}$ remaining).

parts.prompt:
Determine the number of moles of Na
CO
in the original 50.0 mL of solution.
parts.scoring_notes: Moles CaCO$_3$ = 0.93 g / 100.1 g/mol = 0.00929 mol. From the balanced equation: 1 mol Na$_2$CO$_3$ produces 1 mol CaCO$_3$. Moles Na$_2$CO$_3$ = 0.00929 mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of CaCO$_3$: 1 point
wrong_examples: Using molar mass of Ca (40.08 g/mol) instead of CaCO$_3$ (100.1 g/mol).

scoring_points.description: For the correct moles of Na$_2$CO$_3$ = 0.00929 mol using 1:1 stoichiometry: 1 point

required_elements: From balanced equation: 1 mol Na$_2$CO$_3$ : 1 mol CaCO$_3$.
alternatives.correct_example: From the equation, 1 mol Na$_2$CO$_3$ → 1 mol CaCO$_3$. Moles Na$_2$CO$_3$ = 0.00929 mol.

wrong_examples: Using a 2:1 ratio — the balanced equation shows 1 Na$_2$CO$_3$ to 1 CaCO$_3$.
common_traps: Check the stoichiometric ratio from the balanced equation: Na$_2$CO$_3$ : CaCO$_3$ = 1:1.

parts.prompt:
The student realizes that the precipitate was not completely dried and claims that as a result, the
calculated Na
CO
molarity is too low. Do you agree with the student's claim? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Disagree. If the precipitate contains water, its measured mass is greater than the actual mass of dry CaCO$_3$. Using a higher mass in the calculation gives a higher calculated number of moles of CaCO$_3$ → higher moles of Na$_2$CO$_3$ → higher calculated molarity. Therefore, the calculated [Na$_2$CO$_3$] is too HIGH (overestimated), not too low.

scoring_points.description: For disagreeing with correct reasoning: wet precipitate → overestimated mass → higher calculated molarity: 1 point

required_elements: The wet precipitate has more mass than the dry CaCO$_3$ alone → calculated moles CaCO$_3$ (and therefore Na$_2$CO$_3$) are overestimated → calculated [Na$_2$CO$_3$] is too HIGH, not too low.
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. If the precipitate is not fully dried, the recorded mass includes both CaCO$_3$ and water. The inflated mass → inflated moles of CaCO$_3$ → inflated moles of Na$_2$CO$_3$ → calculated molarity is HIGHER than the actual value, not lower.

wrong_examples: Agreeing — undried precipitate has extra mass (water), making the calculated molarity higher, not lower.
Saying no effect — the mass of the precipitate directly determines the calculated moles; any extra mass changes the result.
common_traps: Identify the direction of error: wet precipitate → mass too high → moles CaCO$_3$ too high → moles Na$_2$CO$_3$ too high → [Na$_2$CO$_3$] too HIGH. Student said too low → Disagree.

parts.prompt:
After the precipitate forms and is filtered, the liquid that passed through the filter is tested to see if it can
conduct electricity. What would be observed? Justify your answer.

The student decides to determine the molarity of the same Na
CO
solution using a second method. When
Na
CO
is dissolved in water, $CO_2$−
(aq) hydrolyzes to form HCO
−
(aq), as shown by the following equation.
[HCO -][$OH^-$]
$CO_2$− (aq) + $H_2O$(l)  HCO − (aq) + $OH^-$ (aq) K = 3 = 2.1 × 10$^{-4}$
3 2 3 b [$CO_2$-]
−
parts.scoring_notes: The filtrate conducts electricity. After the reaction and filtration, the solution contains: Na$^+$(aq) (from Na$_2$CO$_3$), excess Ca$^{2+}$(aq) (from excess Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$), and NO$_3^-$(aq) (from Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$). These are all mobile ions → the solution conducts electricity.

scoring_points.description: For stating the filtrate conducts electricity with ionic species justification: 1 point

required_elements: Na$^+$(aq), excess Ca$^{2+}$(aq), and NO$_3^-$(aq) are present in solution — mobile ions conduct electricity.
alternatives.correct_example: The filtrate conducts electricity. After filtration, the solution contains Na$^+$(aq), excess Ca$^{2+}$(aq), and NO$_3^-$(aq). These mobile ions allow electrical charge to flow through the solution.

wrong_examples: Saying no conduction — the CaCO$_3$ precipitate was removed, but many ions remain in solution.
Saying it conducts because of CaCO$_3$ — CaCO$_3$ is insoluble and was filtered out; it does not contribute ions.
common_traps: Electrical conductivity in solution requires mobile ions. After filtering out the precipitate, Na$^+$, excess Ca$^{2+}$, and NO$_3^-$ remain in solution → conductivity.

parts.prompt:
The student decides to first determine [OH ] in the solution, then use that result to calculate the initial
concentration of $CO_2$−
(aq).
−
(i) Identify a laboratory method (not titration) that the student could use to collect data to determine [OH ]
in the solution.
(ii) Explain how the student could use the measured value in part (f)(i) to calculate the initial concentration
of $CO_2$−
(aq). (Do not do any numerical calculations.)
−
parts.scoring_notes: Part (f)(i): Use a pH meter to measure pH, then calculate pOH = 14 − pH, then [OH$^-$] = 10$^{-\text{pOH}}$. Alternatively, use a pH indicator or probe. Part (f)(ii): At equilibrium, [OH$^-$] = [HCO$_3^-$] (from the reaction CO$_3^{2-}$ + H$_2$O ⇌ HCO$_3^-$ + OH$^-$). Since K$_b$ = [HCO$_3^-$][OH$^-$]/[CO$_3^{2-}$], and [HCO$_3^-$] = [OH$^-$] = x, then [CO$_3^{2-}$]$_{eq}$ = x$^2$/K$_b$. The initial [CO$_3^{2-}$] = [CO$_3^{2-}$]$_{eq}$ + x (approximation: ≈ x$^2$/K$_b$ + x). Using x = [OH$^-$] measured and K$_b$, calculate initial [CO$_3^{2-}$].

scoring_points.description: For a valid non-titration laboratory method to determine [OH$^-$]: 1 point

required_elements: Use a pH meter to measure pH; calculate [OH$^-$] from pH via pOH = 14 − pH, [OH$^-$] = 10$^{-\text{pOH}}$. OR use a conductivity meter (with calibration). OR use a universal indicator.
alternatives.correct_example: Measure the pH of the solution using a pH meter. Calculate pOH = 14.00 − pH, then [OH$^-$] = 10$^{-\text{pOH}}$.

wrong_examples: Suggesting titration — the problem specifically excludes titration.
Saying 'add an indicator' without specifying how to get a quantitative [OH$^-$] value.
common_traps: pH meter gives a quantitative pH value → pOH = 14 − pH → [OH$^-$] = 10$^{-\text{pOH}}$. This is the most straightforward non-titration method.

scoring_points.description: For a valid explanation of using [OH$^-$] and K$_b$ to calculate initial [CO$_3^{2-}$]: 1 point

required_elements: At equilibrium: [OH$^-$] = [HCO$_3^-$] = x (from stoichiometry of the hydrolysis equilibrium).
alternatives.correct_example: From the stoichiometry CO$_3^{2-}$ + H$_2$O ⇌ HCO$_3^-$ + OH$^-$: [HCO$_3^-$] = [OH$^-$] = x at equilibrium. Substitute into K$_b$: K$_b$ = x$^2$/[CO$_3^{2-}$]$_{eq}$ → [CO$_3^{2-}$]$_{eq}$ = x$^2$/K$_b$. Initial [CO$_3^{2-}$] = [CO$_3^{2-}$]$_{eq}$ + x = x$^2$/K$_b$ + x.

wrong_examples: Not using K$_b$ at all — the equilibrium expression is needed to relate [OH$^-$] back to [CO$_3^{2-}$].
Saying initial [CO$_3^{2-}$] = [OH$^-$] directly — that would only be true if 100% of CO$_3^{2-}$ reacted, which it doesn't.

parts.prompt:
In the original Na
CO
solution at equilibrium, is the concentration of HCO
(aq) greater than, less than, or
equal to the concentration of $CO_2$−
(aq) ? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Less than. The K$_b$ = 2.1 × 10$^{-4}$ is small, indicating that the hydrolysis reaction proceeds only to a small extent. At equilibrium, only a small fraction of CO$_3^{2-}$ has reacted. Therefore [CO$_3^{2-}$] >> [HCO$_3^-$]; [HCO$_3^-$] is less than [CO$_3^{2-}$].

scoring_points.description: For 'less than' with small K$_b$ justification: 1 point

required_elements: K$_b$ = 2.1 × 10$^{-4}$ is small → equilibrium lies far to the left → only a small fraction of CO$_3^{2-}$ hydrolyzes → [HCO$_3^-$] << [CO$_3^{2-}$].
alternatives.correct_example: Less than. K$_b$ = 2.1 × 10$^{-4}$ << 1, indicating the hydrolysis equilibrium lies far toward the reactants (CO$_3^{2-}$ predominates). Only a small fraction of CO$_3^{2-}$ reacts with water to form HCO$_3^-$. Therefore [HCO$_3^-$] < [CO$_3^{2-}$] at equilibrium.

wrong_examples: Saying [HCO$_3^-$] > [CO$_3^{2-}$] — K$_b$ is small, so the equilibrium favors reactants (CO$_3^{2-}$ unreacted).
Saying equal — they would only be equal at the half-equivalence point of a titration or if K = 1.
common_traps: Small K (K << 1) → equilibrium favors reactants → mostly unreacted CO$_3^{2-}$ → [CO$_3^{2-}$] >> [HCO$_3^-$] → HCO$_3^-$ concentration is less.

parts.prompt:
The student needs to make a $CO_2$−
/HCO
−
buffer. Is the Na
CO
solution suitable for making a buffer
with a pH of 6? Explain why or why not.
parts.scoring_notes: No. A buffer works effectively when the pH is close to the pK$_a$ of the weak acid component. HCO$_3^-$ acts as the weak acid in the CO$_3^{2-}$/HCO$_3^-$ buffer: K$_a$(HCO$_3^-$) = K$_w$/K$_b$(CO$_3^{2-}$) = 1.0 × 10$^{-14}$/2.1 × 10$^{-4}$ = 4.8 × 10$^{-11}$. pK$_a$ = −log(4.8 × 10$^{-11}$) ≈ 10.3. This is far from pH = 6. A buffer is effective only within about ±1 pH unit of its pK$_a$. The CO$_3^{2-}$/HCO$_3^-$ system has pK$_a$ ≈ 10.3, not near 6, so it cannot buffer at pH 6.

scoring_points.description: For 'No' with pK$_a$ of HCO$_3^-$ far from 6 justification: 1 point

required_elements: pK$_a$ of HCO$_3^-$ ≈ 10.3 (calculated from K$_w$/K$_b$), which is far from 6. Buffers are effective only near their pK$_a$ (±1 unit).
alternatives.correct_example: No. Effective buffers work near their pK$_a$. The CO$_3^{2-}$/HCO$_3^-$ buffer has a pK$_a$ (of HCO$_3^-$ as the weak acid) = 14 − pK$_b$ = 14 − 3.68 ≈ 10.3. This is approximately 4 pH units away from the target pH of 6, far outside the effective buffering range of ±1 pH unit. The Na$_2$CO$_3$ solution is NOT suitable for a pH 6 buffer.

wrong_examples: Saying yes — the pK$_a$ of the HCO$_3^-$/CO$_3^{2-}$ system is ~10.3, not near 6.
Using K$_b$ as pK$_a$ directly — must convert: pK$_a$ = 14 − pK$_b$.
common_traps: Buffer effectiveness: pH ≈ pK$_a$ ± 1. For CO$_3^{2-}$/HCO$_3^-$: K$_a$(HCO$_3^-$) = K$_w$/K$_b$(CO$_3^{2-}$) ≈ 4.8 × 10$^{-11}$, pK$_a$ ≈ 10.3. pH 6 is far outside this range.
